text: What is the purpose of a Mongoose Schema?
options: To define the shape and validation rules for documents in application code
correctAnswer: To define the shape and validation rules for documents in application code
explanation: A Schema defines field types, validation, defaults, and other constraints at the application layer. Unlike PostgreSQL's CREATE TABLE, the schema is in your JS code, not the database.
hint: It defines the 'shape' of your documents.

text: In Mongoose, what is a Model?
options: A compiled version of a Schema — the interface for querying and creating documents
correctAnswer: A compiled version of a Schema — the interface for querying and creating documents
explanation: mongoose.model('Book', bookSchema) creates a Model class. You use the Model for all CRUD: Book.find(), Book.create(), Book.findByIdAndUpdate(), etc.
hint: Model = compiled Schema = your query interface.

text: Which is the correct way to define a required string field 'title' in a Mongoose schema?
correctAnswer: { title: { type: String, required: true } }
explanation: Mongoose field definitions use { type: SchemaType, options... }. required: true means Mongoose throws a ValidationError if you try to save without this field.
hint: type and required are both properties of the field config object.

text: What does the 'default' option do in a Mongoose schema field?
options: Sets a fallback value when the field is not provided on insert
Sets the field as the primary key
Validates the field against a default regex
correctAnswer: Sets a fallback value when the field is not provided on insert
explanation: { inStock: { type: Boolean, default: true } } means if you create a book without specifying inStock, it defaults to true.
hint: Like DEFAULT in SQL column definitions.

text: What does Book.create({ title: '1984', price: 12.99 }) do?
options: Validates and inserts a new document into the books collection
correctAnswer: Validates and inserts a new document into the books collection
explanation: create() is a shorthand for new Book({...}).save(). It validates the data against the schema, then inserts it into MongoDB.
hint: Model.create() = validate + insert.

text: What does the 'trim' option do in a Mongoose String field?
options: Automatically removes leading/trailing whitespace before saving
correctAnswer: Automatically removes leading/trailing whitespace before saving
explanation: { type: String, trim: true } runs String.trim() on the value before validation and saving. Prevents accidental spaces in names, emails, etc.
hint: Like JavaScript's .trim() method, applied automatically.

text: How do you query for a document by its _id in Mongoose?
correctAnswer: Book.findById(id)
explanation: findById() is a Mongoose shortcut for findOne({ _id: id }). It accepts the id as a string or ObjectId and automatically casts it.
hint: Mongoose has a dedicated findById shortcut.

text: In a Mongoose schema, how do you define an array of strings (like genres)?
correctAnswer: { genres: [String] }
explanation: [String] is Mongoose's shorthand for an array of strings. You can also write { type: [String] }.
hint: Put the type inside square brackets.

text: What does Book.findByIdAndUpdate(id, update, { new: true }) return?
correctAnswer: The document AFTER the update
explanation: By default, findByIdAndUpdate returns the document BEFORE the update. Pass { new: true } to return the updated document instead.
hint: new: true = return the new (updated) version.

text: What happens when you call save() on a Mongoose document with an invalid field (e.g., price: -5 when min: 0)?
options: A ValidationError is thrown and the document is NOT saved
The field is set to the minimum value automatically
correctAnswer: A ValidationError is thrown and the document is NOT saved
explanation: Mongoose validates the document before saving. If validation fails (wrong type, min/max violation, required missing), it throws a ValidationError and the insert is aborted.
hint: Validation runs before the DB operation.

text: What is a Mongoose pre-save hook used for?
options: Running code after a document is saved
Running code before a document is validated and saved
correctAnswer: Running code before a document is validated and saved
explanation: Pre hooks run before the operation. schema.pre('save', ...) runs before save/create. Common uses: hash passwords, normalize data, set computed fields.
hint: Pre = before the operation.

text: What is a Mongoose virtual?
options: A field with a virtual validator
A computed property that exists in the model but is NOT stored in MongoDB
An index that maps to a virtual collection
A cached version of a document
correctAnswer: A computed property that exists in the model but is NOT stored in MongoDB
explanation: Virtuals are computed on-the-fly from other fields. Example: a fullName virtual that concatenates firstName + lastName. Not stored in the database — recalculated every time.
hint: Computed but not persisted.

text: Complete this Mongoose query to find all books with inStock: true, return only title and price, sorted by price:
codeTemplate: await Book.find({ inStock: true }).______("title price").______({ price: 1 })
correctAnswer: select|sort
explanation: .select('title price') is Mongoose's field projection (like the second argument to find()). .sort({ price: 1 }) sorts ascending.
hint: Mongoose uses .select() for projection and .sort() for ordering.

text: What does { type: String, enum: ['active', 'inactive', 'banned'] } do in a Mongoose schema?
options: Creates an index on those values
Restricts the field to only those allowed string values
Sets the default to the first enum value
correctAnswer: Restricts the field to only those allowed string values
explanation: enum validates that the field value is one of the specified options. Attempting to save any other value throws a ValidationError.
hint: Only these values are allowed.

text: What does the .lean() method do in a Mongoose query?
options: Reduces the number of fields returned
Returns plain JavaScript objects instead of Mongoose Documents — faster but no instance methods
correctAnswer: Returns plain JavaScript objects instead of Mongoose Documents — faster but no instance methods
explanation: .lean() skips the Mongoose Document wrapper, returning plain JS objects. Significantly faster for read-heavy operations where you don't need methods like .save() on the result.
hint: lean = plain JS, faster, no document methods.

text: You want to hash a password before saving a user. Where should this logic go in Mongoose?
options: In the route handler before calling save()
In a pre('save') hook on the user schema
correctAnswer: In a pre('save') hook on the user schema
explanation: Schema.pre('save', async function() { this.password = await hash(this.password); }) runs automatically before every save. This keeps the hashing logic in the model, not scattered in route handlers.
hint: Pre-save = before every save, automatically.

text: Define a Mongoose virtual called 'discountedPrice' that returns price * 0.9:
correctAnswer: virtual|get
explanation: schema.virtual('fieldName').get(function() {...}) defines a virtual getter. Use a regular function (not arrow function) so 'this' refers to the document.
hint: virtual() then get().

text:
Translate this Mongoose-equivalent of a SQL query:
SELECT * FROM books WHERE author = 'Murakami' ORDER BY published_year DESC LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20;
correctAnswer: Book.find({ authorName: 'Murakami' }).sort('-publishedYear').skip(20).limit(10)
explanation: Mongoose allows .sort('-fieldName') as a shorthand for descending sort. .skip(20) = OFFSET 20, .limit(10) = LIMIT 10. All chained fluently.
hint: Minus prefix on field name = descending in Mongoose sort.

text: What is the difference between schema-level validation and custom validators in Mongoose?
options: No difference — all validators are custom
Schema-level: built-in (required, min, max, enum). Custom: { validate: { validator: fn, message: '...' } } for complex business rules
Custom validators run before schema validators
Custom validators are only for async operations
correctAnswer: Schema-level: built-in (required, min, max, enum). Custom: { validate: { validator: fn, message: '...' } } for complex business rules
explanation: Built-in validators cover common cases. Custom validators let you write any logic — async calls to check uniqueness, cross-field validation, etc.
hint: Built-in for common cases, custom for complex rules.

text: A Mongoose pre('save') hook uses a regular function — why should you NOT use an arrow function?
options: Arrow functions don't have their own 'this' — you can't access the document via 'this'
Arrow functions throw an error in pre hooks
correctAnswer: Arrow functions don't have their own 'this' — you can't access the document via 'this'
explanation: In a Mongoose hook, 'this' refers to the document being saved. Arrow functions inherit 'this' from the surrounding scope (not the document). Always use function() {} in Mongoose hooks.
hint: Arrow functions lexically bind 'this' — breaks Mongoose's pattern.
